course_info.title: Cybersecurity Internship
course_info.meeting_schedule: Varies by placement - minimum 20 hours per week

learning_objectives: Understand internship learning objectives and evaluation criteria
Set professional goals aligned with career aspirations
Establish communication protocols with site supervisor and faculty advisor

lecture_notes.introduction: The cybersecurity internship provides real-world experience in a professional security environment. This orientation sets expectations for professionalism, learning, and documentation.
key_concepts: Internship learning agreement: objectives, activities, evaluation
Professional conduct in security environments: NDAs, ethics, confidentiality
Journal and portfolio documentation requirements
Biweekly check-in protocol with faculty advisor
Site supervisor evaluation criteria
lecture_notes.demonstration: Review of learning agreement template and past intern portfolios that demonstrate effective documentation

review.value: Defines specific, measurable learning objectives co-developed by student, site supervisor, and faculty advisor. Activities are mapped to cybersecurity competency domains.
Interns will have access to sensitive security information. NDAs must be signed. Internship journal entries must be sanitized to remove client-specific details.

class_schedule.topic: Professional Development: Resume, Certifications, and Career Paths
learning_objectives: Identify cybersecurity career paths and specialization areas
Plan certification roadmaps aligned with career goals
Develop professional portfolio and resume for security positions
readings: CompTIA, ISC2, and SANS certification roadmaps

lecture_notes.introduction: The internship is a launching pad for your career. Understanding the cybersecurity job market and positioning yourself with the right skills and certifications is essential.
key_concepts: NICE Workforce Framework: cybersecurity work roles and KSAs
Career paths: SOC analyst, penetration tester, GRC analyst, security engineer, CISO
Certification roadmaps: CompTIA (Security+, CySA+), ISC2 (CISSP), SANS (GIAC)
Professional networking: conferences, local chapters, LinkedIn
Portfolio development: projects, certifications, contributions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping individual career goals to NICE framework roles and developing a 2-year certification and skill development plan

review.value: SOC Analyst (Tier 1) -> SOC Analyst (Tier 2) -> Incident Responder -> IR Lead -> SOC Manager. Or: Vulnerability Analyst -> Penetration Tester -> Red Team Lead. Choose specialization early.
Security+ is the baseline. CySA+ or GIAC certs add depth. CISSP requires 5 years experience but demonstrates breadth. Certifications open doors; skills keep the job.

learning_objectives: Reflect on the onboarding experience and initial observations
Identify gaps between academic preparation and workplace requirements
Adjust learning objectives based on initial placement experience

lecture_notes.introduction: The first weeks of an internship involve rapid learning about the organization's security posture, tools, and culture. Structured reflection helps extract maximum learning from this period.
key_concepts: Onboarding observations: tools, processes, team structure
Gap analysis: what the curriculum taught vs what the workplace requires
Adapting to organizational security culture
Building relationships with team members and mentors
Adjusting learning agreement if needed
lecture_notes.demonstration: Group discussion of onboarding experiences, common themes, and strategies for addressing skill gaps
setup_requirements: Journal entries from first two weeks

review.value: Students often find gaps in: specific tool proficiency (Splunk, CrowdStrike, Palo Alto), business communication skills, regulatory knowledge for the specific industry, and understanding of organizational bureaucracy.
Do not wait to be assigned tasks. Ask questions, volunteer for projects, request access to documentation, and shadow experienced team members during incidents.

learning_objectives: Identify a meaningful project within the internship placement
Develop a project plan that delivers value to the organization
Align the project with academic learning objectives

lecture_notes.introduction: The best internships include a defined project that delivers tangible value to the organization. This project becomes the centerpiece of your portfolio and internship evaluation.
key_concepts: Project identification: solve a real problem the team faces
Scoping for 10-week timeline within the internship
Stakeholder management: supervisor, team, faculty advisor
Documentation and knowledge transfer plan
Measuring project impact and success criteria
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students present potential project ideas and receive feedback on scope and feasibility

review.value: Automating a manual process, building a dashboard, writing detection rules, creating runbooks, conducting a security assessment, developing training materials. Must deliver value that persists after the internship.
Everything you build must be documented well enough for someone else to maintain it. Code must be commented, dashboards must have documentation, processes must have runbooks.

learning_objectives: Present mid-internship progress against learning objectives
Discuss challenges and professional development observations
Receive feedback from faculty advisor and adjust plans

lecture_notes.introduction: The mid-internship review assesses progress toward learning objectives and provides an opportunity to course-correct for the remaining weeks.
key_concepts: Self-assessment against learning agreement objectives
Supervisor feedback integration
Project progress review
Professional development observations and growth areas
Adjustment of objectives for remaining weeks
lecture_notes.demonstration: Structured mid-internship presentations with peer and faculty feedback

review.value: Honest reflection on: technical skills gained, professional skills developed, challenges encountered, relationships built, and areas needing more focus in the remaining weeks.

Common feedback themes: initiative (do they seek work or wait?), quality (is their work thorough?), communication (do they ask questions and report progress?), professionalism (reliability, attitude).

class_schedule.topic: Internship Wrap-Up: Portfolio and Presentation Preparation
learning_objectives: Compile internship portfolio with sanitized work samples
Prepare final presentation for faculty and peers
Develop professional connections for post-graduation employment

lecture_notes.introduction: The internship conclusion should produce a professional portfolio, a strong reference from your supervisor, and ideally a path to full-time employment.
key_concepts: Portfolio contents: project deliverables, learning reflections, skill evidence
Sanitization: removing confidential organizational details
Final presentation: what you did, what you learned, what you recommend
Exit interviews: providing value feedback to the organization
Maintaining professional relationships post-internship
lecture_notes.demonstration: Portfolio review workshop and presentation practice session

review.value: Showcase 3-5 key accomplishments with context (problem, approach, result). Include metrics where possible: 'Automated vulnerability scan triage reducing analyst time by 40%.' Sanitize all client details.

Request a LinkedIn recommendation from your supervisor. Maintain the relationship: occasional professional updates, congratulations on achievements, sharing relevant articles. These connections are career-long assets.

major_assignments.title: Cybersecurity Internship Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Complete a professional cybersecurity internship with documented learning outcomes, a workplace project, and a comprehensive portfolio demonstrating applied cybersecurity competencies.
objectives: Apply cybersecurity knowledge in a professional environment
Complete a meaningful project delivering value to the organization
Present internship experience and outcomes to faculty and peers
deliverables: Final portfolio with work samples and reflections
Final presentation to faculty and peers

criteria: Project delivered tangible value to the organization
Thoughtful reflection connecting experience to coursework
Suitable for use in job applications
Professional performance meeting or exceeding supervisor expectations
Meaningful project delivering real value to the organization
Comprehensive documentation of learning and growth
Professional portfolio suitable for career advancement

supervisor_evaluation.description: Site supervisor assessment of professionalism, technical skills, and contribution

project_deliverable.description: Quality and impact of the workplace project

journal_and_portfolio.description: Documentation of learning, reflection, and professional development

final_presentation.description: Presentation of internship experience and outcomes

faculty_assessment.description: Faculty advisor assessment of overall performance and engagement

course_policies.attendance: Students must maintain the minimum 20 hours per week at their internship site. Biweekly check-ins with faculty advisor are mandatory.
course_policies.late_submissions: Journal entries are due weekly. Late submissions are penalized 5% per day. Final portfolio due on the last day of classes.
course_policies.collaboration: The internship is individual. Peer support during biweekly check-ins is encouraged.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All journal entries and portfolio materials must be your own work. Supervisor evaluations are confidential between the supervisor and faculty advisor.
course_policies.confidentiality: Respect all NDAs and confidentiality agreements with the internship organization. Journal entries and portfolio materials must be sanitized. When in doubt, consult your supervisor before including any organizational detail.
